section.title: The Devastation of the Earth
section.sectionRationale: This chapter opens what scholars often call the Isaiah apocalypse, chapters 24 to 27, where the prophet's gaze widens beyond any single nation to the whole earth and beyond any single historical crisis toward God's final judgment. The oracles against individual nations, chapters 13 to 23, now give way to a vision of universal devastation, universal guilt, and the LORD reigning gloriously on Mount Zion.
section.thesis: The LORD lays waste the whole earth because its people have broken the everlasting covenant, yet even amid this universal devastation scattered voices break out in praise, glory to the Righteous One, anticipating the day the LORD reigns openly on Mount Zion before His elders.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the LORD twisting and emptying the earth; the earth mourning under a curse for breaking the everlasting covenant; the city of chaos shattered and joy exiled; scattered voices of praise amid terror, glory to the Righteous One; and the earth split open as the LORD comes to reign on Zion.

units.label: The LORD lays waste the earth

verses.text: Behold, the LORD lays waste the earth and leaves it in ruins. He will twist its surface and scatter its inhabitants,
people and priest alike, servant and master, maid and mistress, buyer and seller, lender and borrower, creditor and debtor.
The earth will be utterly laid waste and thoroughly plundered. For the LORD has spoken this word.

units.label: Mourning under a curse for a broken covenant

verses.text: The earth mourns and withers; the world languishes and fades; the exalted of the earth waste away.
The earth is defiled by its people; they have transgressed the laws; they have overstepped the decrees and broken the {{beritOlam|everlasting covenant}}.
Therefore a curse has consumed the earth, and its inhabitants must bear the guilt; the earth's dwellers have been burned, and only a few survive.
The new wine dries up, the vine withers. All the merrymakers now groan.
The joyful tambourines have ceased; the noise of revelers has stopped; the joyful harp is silent.
They no longer sing and drink wine; strong drink is bitter to those who consume it.
The city of chaos is shattered; every house is closed to entry.
In the streets they cry out for wine. All joy turns to gloom; rejoicing is exiled from the land.
The city is left in ruins; its gate is reduced to rubble.
So will it be on the earth and among the nations, like a harvested olive tree, like a gleaning after a grape harvest.

units.label: Voices of praise amid the terror

verses.text: They raise their voices, they shout for joy; from the west they proclaim the majesty of the LORD.
Therefore glorify the LORD in the east. Extol the name of the LORD, the God of Israel in the islands of the sea.
From the ends of the earth we hear singing: “Glory to the Righteous One.” But I said, “I am wasting away! I am wasting away! Woe is me.” The treacherous betray; the treacherous deal in treachery.
Terror and pit and snare await you, O dweller of the earth.
Whoever flees the sound of panic will fall into the pit, and whoever climbs from the pit will be caught in the snare. For the windows of heaven are open, and the foundations of the earth are shaken.

units.label: The earth split open, the LORD reigns on Zion

verses.text: The earth is utterly broken apart, the earth is split open, the earth is shaken violently.
The earth staggers like a drunkard and sways like a shack. Earth's rebellion weighs it down, and it falls, never to rise again.
In that day the LORD will punish the host of heaven above and the kings of the earth below.
They will be gathered together like prisoners in a pit. They will be confined to a dungeon and punished after many days.
The moon will be confounded and the sun will be ashamed; for the LORD of Hosts will reign on Mount Zion and in Jerusalem, and before His elders with great glory.

beritOlam.gloss: An everlasting covenant, a phrase used elsewhere for God's covenants with Noah, Abraham, and David. Its breaking here by the earth's whole population, not Israel alone, suggests a violation of the moral order woven into creation itself, the reason the whole earth, not one nation, now lies under judgment.

basic.context: Isaiah's gaze suddenly widens from individual nations to the entire earth. Every class of person, priest and people, master and servant, buyer and seller, is swept into the same devastation, because the earth's inhabitants have broken an everlasting covenant. Yet even in this bleak vision, scattered voices break out from every direction singing glory to the Righteous One, and the chapter ends with the LORD reigning gloriously on Mount Zion.
basic.hermeneutics: Chapters 24 to 27 are often called the Isaiah apocalypse because their scope moves beyond any single historical judgment toward a more universal, cosmic horizon, though scholars debate exactly how much this looks to near history and how much to the final end. This chapter's vision of a completely leveled city and a whole earth judged for breaking a covenant likely draws together both, a near historical judgment on human pride and a genuine foretaste of the final judgment and reign of God, and readers should hold its details with real interpretive humility rather than forcing a single timetable onto it.
basic.originalLanguages: The word eretz, earth or land, recurs so often that it becomes almost a drumbeat through the chapter, insisting this judgment is not local but total. At its center stands the charge that humanity broke the berit olam, the everlasting covenant, language elsewhere reserved for God's covenants with Noah, Abraham, and David, suggesting a violation of creation's basic moral order.
basic.typology: The LORD reigning openly on Mount Zion before His elders with great glory, verse 23, anticipates the full, visible reign of Christ, who will judge and reign over all the earth. The everlasting covenant broken by humanity here anticipates the new and better everlasting covenant God establishes through Christ's blood, Hebrews 13:20.
basic.christ: The chapter's closing vision of the LORD reigning gloriously on Mount Zion before His elders anticipates Christ's coming kingdom, when He who judges the earth in righteousness will reign visibly and forever, Revelation 11:15. Where humanity broke the everlasting covenant and brought the earth's curse, Christ establishes an everlasting covenant that cannot be broken, sealed in His own blood, Hebrews 13:20.
basic.god: God's judgment here reaches everyone, without regard to social status, because the underlying offense, breaking His everlasting covenant, belongs to humanity as a whole, not one class or nation. Yet even in this sweeping judgment He is not silent toward praise; voices rise from every direction glorifying Him as the Righteous One, and His reign on Zion is the chapter's final, glorious word.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 9:16 and Genesis 17:7 establish earlier everlasting covenants that this chapter's broken covenant echoes and intensifies. Romans 8:19-22 describes creation groaning under the weight of human sin, awaiting redemption, much like this chapter's mourning earth. Hebrews 13:20 names the new covenant sealed in Christ's blood as eternal. Revelation 11:15 announces the final reign of Christ, echoing this chapter's vision of the LORD reigning on Zion.
basic.oneStory: This chapter widens Isaiah's vision from individual nations to the whole earth, judged for breaking God's everlasting covenant, yet ends in praise and the LORD's glorious reign on Zion. It is a foretaste of the final judgment and kingdom of Christ, who both judges the earth's rebellion and establishes an everlasting covenant that will never be broken.

context.literary: The opening movement of the so-called Isaiah apocalypse, chapters 24 to 27, marked by a shift from named nations to the whole earth, eretz, and structured around waves of devastation punctuated by unexpected bursts of praise, culminating in the LORD's open reign on Mount Zion.
context.historical: While rooted in Isaiah's eighth century context and echoing the devastations of Assyrian and later Babylonian conquest, the chapter's universal scope, the whole earth rather than one city or nation, suggests it looks beyond any single historical event toward a more ultimate horizon, a pattern common in apocalyptic literature where near and far judgments overlap.
context.canonical: Introduces themes developed through chapters 25 to 27, including the swallowing up of death, 25:8, cited in 1 Corinthians 15:54, and Israel's future resurrection, 26:19, situating this material among Scripture's earliest and most vivid anticipations of final, cosmic judgment and resurrection hope.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To declare that the LORD's judgment ultimately reaches the whole earth and every class of person, because humanity as a whole has broken His everlasting covenant, while also promising that His judgment will end in His own glorious, universally acknowledged reign.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a sweeping judgment and its aftermath rather than prescribing specific actions; its abiding truths are the universality of human guilt before God, the certainty of His judgment, and the sure hope of His eventual reign, not a precise timetable for these events.

debates.question: Does Isaiah 24 describe a specific historical judgment, such as the fall of a particular ancient city or empire, or does it look to the final, cosmic judgment at the end of history?

views.label: A near historical judgment, likely on Babylon or another specific city
views.case: The city of chaos, verses 10 to 12, may refer to a particular ancient city's fall, such as Babylon's, with the chapter's cosmic language functioning as poetic hyperbole common in prophetic judgment oracles.

views.label: A vision reaching toward the final, universal judgment
views.case: The chapter's scope, the whole earth rather than a single city, its language of the everlasting covenant broken by humanity as such, and its place opening the wider so-called Isaiah apocalypse, chapters 24 to 27, suggest it looks beyond any single historical event to God's final judgment and reign.

debates.bearing: Both readings can be held together: the prophetic pattern often uses a near historical judgment as a preview and guarantee of the final, cosmic one, so that this chapter's devastation is both a real warning within history and a genuine foretaste of the last judgment, meriting real interpretive humility about the precise horizon intended.

study.originalLanguages: The word eretz, earth or land, is repeated so insistently that its very frequency signals the chapter's shift from local to universal judgment. The charge that humanity broke the berit olam, everlasting covenant, borrows language elsewhere used of God's covenants with Noah and Abraham, implying that the moral order woven into creation itself, not merely one nation's specific covenant, has been violated.

typology.type: The everlasting covenant broken by humanity, bringing a curse on the earth
typology.fulfillment: The new and better everlasting covenant established through Christ's blood, which cannot be broken, Hebrews 13:20.

typology.type: The LORD reigning openly on Mount Zion before His elders with great glory
typology.fulfillment: Christ's visible, universal reign, announced in Revelation 11:15, when the kingdoms of the world become the kingdom of our Lord and of His Christ.

study.christ: This chapter's final vision, the LORD of Hosts reigning on Mount Zion and in Jerusalem before His elders with great glory, anticipates the open, universal reign of Christ, who will judge the earth's rebellion fully and reign forever, Revelation 11:15. Where humanity's breaking of the everlasting covenant brought a curse consuming the earth, Christ inaugurates a new and better everlasting covenant, sealed in His own blood, that will never be broken or revoked, Hebrews 13:20, so that the curse this chapter describes finds its ultimate answer in Him.
study.god: God's judgment in this chapter falls with total impartiality, on priest and people, master and servant alike, because the offense, breaking His everlasting covenant, belongs to humanity as such, not to any single class or nation. Yet even amid devastating judgment He receives praise from the ends of the earth, and His reign on Zion is the chapter's ultimate, glorious destination, showing that judgment serves His larger purpose of being openly acknowledged as King.

crossReferences.note: The everlasting covenant with Noah, part of the covenant background this chapter's broken everlasting covenant echoes and intensifies.
Creation groaning under the weight of human sin while awaiting redemption, closely paralleling this chapter's mourning, withering earth.
The new, unbreakable everlasting covenant sealed in Christ's blood, the answer to the broken everlasting covenant of Isaiah 24:5.
The kingdoms of the world become the kingdom of the Lord and His Christ, the final fulfillment of the LORD reigning on Zion before His elders.

study.oneStory: This chapter widens Isaiah's vision from individual nations to the whole earth's guilt before God for breaking His everlasting covenant, yet even here praise breaks out and the LORD's reign on Zion stands as the final word. It is an early, vivid preview of the whole Bible's storyline moving through universal judgment toward the everlasting covenant and glorious reign secured for us in Christ.

quiz.q: Who is affected by the devastation described in verses 1 to 3?

options.text: Every class of person, without exception, priest and people, master and servant alike.
options.note: Verse 2 deliberately lists paired social classes to show total, impartial devastation.

options.text: Only the wealthy ruling classes.
options.note: The opposite; the text emphasizes that no class is exempt, not the wealthy alone.

options.text: Only foreign nations, not Israel or Judah.
options.note: The chapter's scope is the whole earth, not a foreign-nations-only judgment as in prior chapters.

options.text: Only those who worship idols.
options.note: Idolatry is part of the broader guilt named later, but verse 2 specifically stresses universal social scope, not a single sin category.

quiz.q: What underlying offense does verse 5 identify as the reason for this devastation?

options.text: Humanity has broken the everlasting covenant and overstepped God's decrees.
options.note: Verse 5 names this exact charge as the root cause.

options.text: A specific king failed to pay tribute to Assyria.
options.note: Not the stated reason; the charge is a broken covenant with God, not political tribute.

options.text: A famine caused by poor harvests.
options.note: Agricultural collapse is described as a result, verses 7 to 9, not the root cause given in verse 5.

options.text: A border dispute between neighboring cities.
options.note: Not addressed in this passage; the stated cause is covenant-breaking, a moral and spiritual offense.

quiz.q: What surprising element appears in verses 14 to 16 amid the devastation?

options.text: Voices from every direction singing praise, glory to the Righteous One.
options.note: Verses 14 to 16 describe exactly this outburst of praise amid the judgment.

options.text: A total absence of any human response at all.
options.note: The opposite; there is a vivid, vocal response of praise described.

options.text: A rebellion against the LORD by the survivors.
options.note: Not what is described; the response given is worship, not rebellion.

options.text: A new alliance formed between all the surviving nations.
options.note: Not the content of these verses; the specific detail is praise directed to the LORD, not a political alliance.

quiz.q: How does the chapter conclude, in verse 23?

options.text: The LORD of Hosts will reign on Mount Zion and in Jerusalem with great glory.
options.note: Verse 23 gives this exact, glorious concluding vision.

options.text: The earth is left permanently empty with no future mentioned.
options.note: The opposite; the chapter ends with a positive vision of the LORD's reign, not emptiness.

options.text: A human king is crowned to rule over the restored earth.
options.note: The reign described is specifically the LORD's own reign, not a human king's.

options.text: The sun and moon are restored to full, undimmed brightness.
options.note: The text says the moon is confounded and the sun ashamed, precisely because the LORD's own glory now outshines them, not that they are restored to normal brightness.

quiz.q: How does the broken everlasting covenant in this chapter connect to the New Testament?

options.text: It anticipates the new, unbreakable everlasting covenant established in Christ's blood.
options.note: Hebrews 13:20 names this new covenant as everlasting and secured in Christ, the answer to the broken covenant here.

options.text: It shows that God abandoned the idea of covenant altogether after this point.
options.note: The opposite; Scripture continues and ultimately fulfills covenant language through Christ.

options.text: It refers only to a covenant between two ancient Near Eastern kings.
options.note: The phrase everlasting covenant elsewhere describes God's covenants with Noah and Abraham, not a purely political treaty.

options.text: It has no connection to any later biblical covenant themes.
options.note: There is a clear canonical link to God's covenant language developed further and ultimately fulfilled in Christ.

questions: This judgment falls on every social class without exception, priest and people, master and servant alike. Are there ways you assume your particular status or role would exempt you from accountability before God?
The chapter names breaking the everlasting covenant as the root cause of the earth's devastation. What covenant commitments, to God or to others, have you been tempted to treat lightly?
Even amid overwhelming devastation, voices rise from every direction singing glory to the Righteous One. Where in your own hard circumstances could you choose to voice praise rather than only lament?

The chapter ends with the LORD reigning gloriously on Mount Zion despite the earth's rebellion. How does the certainty of God's ultimate, visible reign change the way you face uncertainty or chaos in the world today?
Humanity broke God's everlasting covenant, but Christ established a new one that can never be broken. How secure do you feel in that new covenant compared to how you feel about your own faithfulness?

translationNotes.note: Where BSB prints an em dash after scatter its inhabitants, it has been rendered as a comma per house style, since the sentence continues listing the affected classes in verse 2.
The city of chaos likely functions as a representative or symbolic city rather than one specific, named location, standing for human civilization's proud strongholds generally.
Before His elders likely refers to the heavenly council or the elders of Israel witnessing the LORD's glory, an image echoed later in Revelation's twenty four elders around God's throne.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 24:1-23. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
